items.title: Immune dysfunction in Alzheimer disease
items.author: Howard L. Weiner
items.content: The importance of peripheral immunity in Alzheimer disease has gained much traction in recent years, mainly due to multiple genome-wide association studies identifying risk loci associated with genes expressed predominantly in the periphery. This review discusses the importance of peripheral immune cells in shaping brain physiology and their role in Alzheimer disease.

items.title: Mixed selectivity: when neurons stopped looking like specialists
items.author: Fanny Cazettes
items.content: In this Journal Club, Fanny Cazettes highlights a 2013 paper that demonstrated the importance of mixed selectivity for cortical computations.

items.title: The on-site, on-demand, neuronal gene machine
items.author: Matthew L. Kraushar
items.content: In this Journal Club, Matthew Kraushar discusses a study published in 1996 that found a role for local protein translation in hippocampal synaptic plasticity.

items.title: Stem cell-mediated recovery in stroke: partnering with the immune system
items.author: Saef Izzy
items.content: In this Review, Saef Izzy and colleagues examine the therapeutic potential of stem cells in stroke, with a focus on neural and mesenchymal stem cells. They explore how these stem cells interact with brain immune cells to modulate the inflammatory microenvironment, restore blood–brain barrier integrity and promote tissue repair following a stroke.

items.title: Time, space, memory and brain–body rhythms
items.author: György Buzsáki
items.content: Understanding how the brain represents experienced time and how representations of space and time are integrated to form episodic memories has been a goal of much neuroscientific research. In this Perspective, Buzsáki discusses classical and contemporary ideas about time perception and proposes that a hierarchy of brain–body rhythms contributes to our subjective experience of time.

items.title: Programmed axon degeneration: mechanism, inhibition and therapeutic potential
items.author: Lukas J. Neukomm
items.content: Programmed axon degeneration (PAxD) is activated by axotomy to execute the self-destruction of a severed distal axon. It may also be activated by some non-axotomy insults, suggesting it has a role in some neurodegenerative diseases. Here, Loreto and Neukomm review the molecular mechanisms of PAxD, its involvement in disease and its potential as a therapeutic target.

items.title: Integrated recall arousal
items.author: Jake Rogers
items.content: Functional integration of large-scale brain networks in humans mediates emotional arousal-enhanced memory encoding.
